name: Humanity's First Exam classification schema
note: Choose the primary theme, claim, ground, and mode when one is sufficient. Add another only when the passage clearly makes a second argument, and use no more than three in any field. Claims must record whether the passage advances, rejects, considers, or reports them.

themes.label: Human agency
themes.definition: Questions about choice, self-command, and responsibility.

children.label: Free will and automatism
children.definition: Whether conscious choice directs action or merely accompanies prior causes.

children.label: Habit and self-command
children.definition: How habit, attention, desire, and discipline enlarge or diminish a person's command of themselves.

children.label: Moral responsibility
children.definition: When a person can be praised, blamed, punished, or excused for an action.

themes.label: Machines and agency
themes.definition: Questions about what machines are and what powers or standing they may acquire.

children.label: Tools and agents
children.definition: Whether a machine remains an instrument or can acquire purposes of its own.

children.label: Machine minds and made persons
children.definition: Whether a made being can think, feel, will, suffer, or deserve moral standing.

children.label: Dependence, succession, and replacement
children.definition: Whether machines make people dependent, displace human capacities, or succeed humanity.

themes.label: Society and power
themes.definition: Questions about technology as part of labour, government, and collective life.

children.label: Labour and mechanization
children.definition: How machinery changes work, skill, dignity, ownership, and economic dependence.

children.label: Government, measurement, and surveillance
children.definition: How technical and administrative systems classify, predict, watch, or govern people.

children.label: Crowds, media, and public opinion
children.definition: How mass communication, imitation, and collective feeling shape individual judgment.

themes.label: Human futures
themes.definition: Questions about the long-term direction and limits of human development.

children.label: Progress and decline
children.definition: Whether history tends toward improvement, decay, or neither.

children.label: Heredity and biological control
children.definition: Attempts to shape future people through reproduction, inheritance, medicine, or selection.

children.label: Technological catastrophe
children.definition: Human-made powers that threaten societies, civilization, or human survival.

relations.label: advances
relations.definition: The passage argues for or relies upon the claim.

relations.label: rejects
relations.definition: The passage argues against the claim.

relations.label: considers
relations.definition: The passage seriously examines the claim without clearly accepting or rejecting it.

relations.label: reports or quotes
relations.definition: The passage describes another person's position without adopting it.

grounds.label: Consequences and welfare
grounds.definition: The argument turns on benefit, harm, happiness, safety, or aggregate well-being.

grounds.label: Rights and self-government
grounds.definition: The argument turns on liberty, consent, equality, privacy, or a person's authority over their own life.

grounds.label: Character and human development
grounds.definition: The argument asks what habits, capacities, virtues, or forms of life a practice cultivates.

grounds.label: Duty and responsibility
grounds.definition: The argument turns on obligations, accountability, or what one person owes another.

grounds.label: Economic and material power
grounds.definition: The argument turns on ownership, work, wealth, class, institutions, or control of resources.

grounds.label: Scientific or empirical evidence
grounds.definition: The argument appeals to observation, experiment, measurement, or causal explanation.

grounds.label: Nature and evolution
grounds.definition: The argument appeals to natural order, adaptation, inheritance, struggle, or evolutionary development.

grounds.label: Religion or metaphysics
grounds.definition: The argument depends on claims about soul, providence, ultimate purpose, or the nature of reality.

autonomyEffects.label: Enlarges autonomy
autonomyEffects.definition: The passage presents the development as expanding meaningful human choice or capacity.

autonomyEffects.label: Diminishes autonomy
autonomyEffects.definition: The passage presents the development as narrowing meaningful human choice or capacity.

autonomyEffects.label: Conditional or mixed
autonomyEffects.definition: Its effect depends on use, ownership, institutions, or competing gains and losses.

autonomyEffects.label: No judgment offered
autonomyEffects.definition: The passage does not clearly evaluate the effect on human autonomy.

genres.label: Philosophy
genres.definition: A sustained conceptual or moral argument.

genres.label: Science or medicine
genres.definition: Scientific, psychological, medical, or technical writing.

genres.label: Politics or economics
genres.definition: Writing about government, law, political economy, labour, or public policy.

genres.label: Fiction
genres.definition: A novel, story, utopia, dystopia, or other imagined narrative.

genres.label: Journalism
genres.definition: News, magazine, review, or general periodical writing.

genres.label: Public address
genres.definition: A speech, lecture, sermon, manifesto, or public appeal.

genres.label: Personal writing
genres.definition: A letter, diary, memoir, notebook, or other personal record.

modes.label: Analysis
modes.definition: Explains or distinguishes ideas and causes.

modes.label: Prediction
modes.definition: Describes what may or will happen.

modes.label: Warning
modes.definition: Calls attention to a danger or possible loss.

modes.label: Satire
modes.definition: Uses irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to test or criticize a position.

modes.label: Testimony
modes.definition: Uses personal or witnessed experience as evidence.

modes.label: Proposal
modes.definition: Recommends a policy, practice, or course of action.

linkTypes.label: echoes
linkTypes.definition: Makes a meaningfully similar argument without evidence of direct influence.

linkTypes.label: opposes
linkTypes.definition: Takes a contrary position on the same problem.

linkTypes.label: reframes
linkTypes.definition: Preserves a problem while changing its concepts, causes, or stakes.

linkTypes.label: responds directly
linkTypes.definition: Explicitly discusses or answers the other source.

linkTypes.label: influenced by
linkTypes.definition: Documentary evidence establishes a path of influence.
